(**NY 21**) is a [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") extending for about one hundred miles (160.9 km) through the western part of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. The southern terminus of the route is at an intersection with [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417") in the [village of Andover](/wiki/Andover_(village),_New_York "Andover (village), New York"), and its northern terminus is at a junction with [NY 104](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_104 "New York State Route 104") in the town of [Williamson](/wiki/Williamson,_New_York "Williamson, New York"). In between, NY 21 serves the cities of [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York") and [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(city),_New_York "Canandaigua (city), New York") and intersects several major east–west routes, including the [Southern Tier Expressway](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)") (I-86/[NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17")) near Hornell, the [conjoined](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") routes of [U.S. Route 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") (US 20) and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") in Canandaigua, the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90")) in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester_(village),_New_York "Manchester (village), New York"), and [NY 31](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31 "New York State Route 31") in [Palmyra](/wiki/Palmyra_(village),_New_York "Palmyra (village), New York").

**New York State Route 21** (**NY 21**) is a [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") extending for about one hundred miles (160.9 km) through the western part of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. The southern terminus of the route is at an intersection with [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417") in the [village of Andover](/wiki/Andover_(village),_New_York "Andover (village), New York"), and its northern terminus is at a junction with [NY 104](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_104 "New York State Route 104") in the town of [Williamson](/wiki/Williamson,_New_York "Williamson, New York"). In between, NY 21 serves the cities of [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York") and [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(city),_New_York "Canandaigua (city), New York") and intersects several major east–west routes, including the [Southern Tier Expressway](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)") (I-86/[NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17")) near Hornell, the [conjoined](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") routes of [U.S. Route 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") (US 20) and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") in Canandaigua, the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90 "Interstate 90")) in [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester_(village),_New_York "Manchester (village), New York"), and [NY 31](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31 "New York State Route 31") in [Palmyra](/wiki/Palmyra_(village),_New_York "Palmyra (village), New York").

NY 21 originally extended from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in the south to [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") in the north when it was assigned as part of the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"). South of [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York"), the route followed modern [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36"). NY 21 was rerouted to follow its current alignment south of [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York") in the 1950s and truncated on its northern end to [Williamson](/wiki/Williamson,_New_York "Williamson, New York") in 1980. Other changes of local importance, mostly realignments to bypass communities along the route, have also occurred at various points in the route's history. NY 21 originally had an alternate route around [Canandaigua Lake](/wiki/Canandaigua_Lake "Canandaigua Lake"); however, that highway—designated [NY 21A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21A "New York State Route 21A")—was eliminated in the 1940s.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_21&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

### Andover to Naples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_21&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Andover to Naples")]

NY 21 begins at an intersection with [NY 417](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_417 "New York State Route 417") in the [village of Andover](/wiki/Andover_(village),_New_York "Andover (village), New York"). The route heads northward, paralleling the [Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Western_New_York_and_Pennsylvania_Railroad_(2001) "Western New York and Pennsylvania Railroad (2001)") (WNYP) through a rural stretch of eastern [Allegany County](/wiki/Allegany_County,_New_York "Allegany County, New York"). At Alfred Station, located just east of the [village of Alfred](/wiki/Alfred_(village),_New_York "Alfred (village), New York"), the highway meets the eastern terminus of [NY 244](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_244 "New York State Route 244"). NY 21 and the WNYP head northeast through the [village of Almond](/wiki/Almond_(village),_New_York "Almond (village), New York") to the [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York") line, where the road and rail line begin to turn to the southeast toward [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York"). The portion of NY 21 near Almond briefly parallels the [Southern Tier Expressway](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)"), designated Interstate 86 (I-86) and [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17"). East of the county line, NY 21 leaves the vicinity of the expressway and passes through another undeveloped stretch to reach the city of Hornell, where it becomes West Main Street.[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/NY_64_and_NY_21_fork.jpg/250px-NY_64_and_NY_21_fork.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_64_and_NY_21_fork.jpg)

Northbound at the [fork](/wiki/Fork_(road) "Fork (road)") between NY 21 and NY 64 in South Bristol

The highway follows West Main Street for several blocks into the city's downtown district, where it crosses the [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway")'s [Southern Tier Line](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Line "Southern Tier Line") and intersects [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36"). NY 21 turns north onto NY 36, separating from the WNYP and forming a [concurrency](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with NY 36 north along a four-lane divided highway known as the Maple City Bypass. The overlap ends north of the city limits in the town of [Hornellsville](/wiki/Hornellsville,_New_York "Hornellsville, New York"), where NY 21 splits from NY 36 and resumes its northeasterly track. Not far to the north of this point is exit 34 of the Southern Tier Expressway, accessed by way of NY 36. From Hornell, NY 21 heads northeast through progressively less developed areas as it winds its way across the hilly terrain of the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier"). For the next 15 miles (24 km), NY 21 serves only small, roadside hamlets, such as [Fremont](/wiki/Fremont,_Steuben_County,_New_York "Fremont, Steuben County, New York"), Haskinville, and Loon Lake.[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3)

North of Haskinville, NY 21 takes on a more northerly course through the county that eventually brings it to the [village of Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York"), where it intersects [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") at exit 3 south of the village. The interchange also serves as the southern terminus of [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") and is adjacent to the north end of [NY 415](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_415 "New York State Route 415"), which terminates north of the exit at NY 21. NY 15 joins NY 21 north into the center of Wayland, where the two routes separate upon intersecting [NY 63](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_63 "New York State Route 63"). While NY 15 continues north from the village into [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York"), NY 21 leaves Wayland on an east–west alignment, roughly paralleling the northern county line and a [B&H Rail Corporation](/wiki/B%26H_Rail_Corporation "B&H Rail Corporation") line as it runs through a valley surrounding the [Cohocton River](/wiki/Cohocton_River "Cohocton River"). After 6 miles (9.7 km), the road enters the hamlet of [North Cohocton](/wiki/North_Cohocton,_New_York "North Cohocton, New York"), centered around NY 21's junction with the northern terminus of [NY 371](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_371 "New York State Route 371").[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3)

While the river, its valley, and the rail line proceed south from North Cohocton, NY 21 turns to the northeast, ascending out of the valley and finally crossing the county line, at this point bordering [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York"). The route meanders across another area of hilly, undeveloped terrain to the [village of Naples](/wiki/Naples_(village),_New_York "Naples (village), New York"), home to the north end of [NY 53](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_53 "New York State Route 53") and the south end of [NY 245](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_245 "New York State Route 245"). NY 21 meets the two routes at opposite ends of the community, with NY 53 terminating south of the village center and NY 245 beginning to its north. Naples also serves as NY 21's entrance to the [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes") region; the village itself is located in the southernmost part of the [Canandaigua Lake](/wiki/Canandaigua_Lake "Canandaigua Lake") valley.[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3)

### Naples to Williamson

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_21&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Naples to Williamson")]

[![A wide street with two-lane roadways in either direction and older commercial buildings on either side, seen from a grassy median with the American flag flying from a low post in the center](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Downtown_Canandaigua%2C_NY.jpg/250px-Downtown_Canandaigua%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Canandaigua,_NY.jpg)

Main Street in Canandaigua

NY 21 continues north from Naples, running alongside the western edge of the lake valley, and eventually the lake itself. Near Woodville, a hamlet 3 miles (4.8 km) north of Naples, the route briefly enters [Yates County](/wiki/Yates_County,_New_York "Yates County, New York") for just over a half-mile (0.8 km) before reentering Ontario County. It serves lakeside homes and cottages in the town of [South Bristol](/wiki/South_Bristol,_New_York "South Bristol, New York") for another 2 miles (3.2 km), after which the road begins to climb the side of the valley and meander toward the hamlet of Bristol Springs. Here, the road [forks](/wiki/Fork_(road) "Fork (road)"), with [NY 64](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_64 "New York State Route 64") beginning to the left (west) and NY 21 continuing to the right (north). While NY 64 heads northwest to [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York"), NY 21 progresses to the northeast, overlooking Canandaigua Lake on its way through mostly isolated areas of the [town of Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(town),_New_York "Canandaigua (town), New York"). The route eventually reaches the outskirts of the [city of Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(city),_New_York "Canandaigua (city), New York"), where it intersects [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5").[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3)

At this point, NY 21 turns east, joining US 20 and NY 5 as all three routes head around the southwestern portion of the city. The primary portion of the bypass ends about 1.5 miles (2.4 km) later at an intersection with South Main Street; the junction also marks the east end of the overlap between the three routes and the south end of [NY 332](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_332 "New York State Route 332"). NY 21 turns north onto South Main Street, following NY 332 through the city's [historic downtown district](/wiki/Canandaigua_Historic_District "Canandaigua Historic District"). The two routes pass by [426 South Main Street](/wiki/Building_at_426_South_Main_Street "Building at 426 South Main Street"), [Benham House](/wiki/Benham_House "Benham House"), and the city's former [United States Post Office](/wiki/United_States_Post_Office_(Canandaigua,_New_York) "United States Post Office (Canandaigua, New York)"), all properties listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"), before splitting at the junction of Gibson and North Main streets. NY 332 continues north from this point on North Main Street toward [Victor](/wiki/Victor_(village),_New_York "Victor (village), New York") while NY 21 follows Gibson Street northeastward through a residential section of the city. It passes [Sonnenberg Gardens and Mansion State Historic Park](/wiki/Sonnenberg_Gardens_and_Mansion_State_Historic_Park "Sonnenberg Gardens and Mansion State Historic Park") and the city's [VA Hospital](/wiki/VA_Hospital "VA Hospital") before leaving the Canandaigua city limits.[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3)

Outside the Canandaigua city limits, the area surrounding NY 21 becomes rural once again; however, the surrounding terrain is more level than it was south of the city. The route heads northeast through farmland to [Chapin](/wiki/Chapin,_New_York "Chapin, New York"), a small hamlet in the town of [Hopewell](/wiki/Hopewell,_New_York "Hopewell, New York"), where it intersects the south end of [NY 488](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_488 "New York State Route 488") and turns to take on a more northerly course. Not far to the north are the adjacent villages of [Shortsville](/wiki/Shortsville,_New_York "Shortsville, New York") and [Manchester](/wiki/Manchester_(village),_New_York "Manchester (village), New York"), roughly separated by the [Ontario Central Railroad](/wiki/Ontario_Central_Railroad "Ontario Central Railroad"). Here, NY 21 passes under the railroad and intersects both [NY 96](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96 "New York State Route 96") and the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York")) at exit 43 just north of the Manchester village center. Past the Thruway, NY 21 becomes rural in nature once more as it runs across rolling terrain to the [Wayne County](/wiki/Wayne_County,_New_York "Wayne County, New York") line.[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/NY_104_at_NY_21_in_Williamson.jpg/250px-NY_104_at_NY_21_in_Williamson.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_104_at_NY_21_in_Williamson.jpg)

Northern terminus of NY 21 at NY 104 in Williamson

Within Wayne County, the area around NY 21 becomes more developed as it enters the [village of Palmyra](/wiki/Palmyra_(village),_New_York "Palmyra (village), New York"),[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3) where the road is village-maintained from the southern village line to its junction with [NY 31](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31 "New York State Route 31") in the village's [historic center](/wiki/Palmyra_Village_Historic_District "Palmyra Village Historic District").[[4]](#cite_note-palmyra_quad-4) The route briefly overlaps NY 31 along East Main Street before crossing both the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") and the [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation")-owned [Rochester Subdivision](/wiki/Rochester_Subdivision "Rochester Subdivision") on its way out of the village. From Palmyra to [Williamson](/wiki/Williamson,_New_York "Williamson, New York"), the land surrounding NY 21 is predominantly rural in nature. However, midway between the two locations, NY 21 comes close to the town of [Marion](/wiki/Marion,_New_York "Marion, New York"), which it bypasses to the west. Due to this bypass Marion receives much less traffic, and businesses are left to rot, similarly to Radiator Springs in the movie [Cars](/wiki/Cars_(film) "Cars (film)"). In Williamson, NY 21 passes through the town center at a junction with [Ridge Road](/wiki/Ridge_Road_(Western_New_York) "Ridge Road (Western New York)") before ending at an intersection with [NY 104](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_104 "New York State Route 104") in a more commercial section of the town.[[3]](#cite_note-nymap07-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_21&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_21&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Origins")]

Prior to the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), the path of modern NY 21 north of [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(city),_New_York "Canandaigua (city), New York") was part of an old [Native American](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") trail connecting [Seneca Lake](/wiki/Seneca_Lake_(New_York) "Seneca Lake (New York)") to [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") by way of [Canandaigua Lake](/wiki/Canandaigua_Lake "Canandaigua Lake"). The trail began in the vicinity of [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva_(city),_New_York "Geneva (city), New York") and went west to Canandaigua on a path now occupied by [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5"). It continued north from Canandaigua, passing through the village of [Palmyra](/wiki/Palmyra_(village),_New_York "Palmyra (village), New York") and the hamlet of [Marion](/wiki/Marion,_New_York "Marion, New York") before reaching the Lake Ontario shoreline at [Pultneyville](/wiki/Pultneyville,_New_York "Pultneyville, New York"). Construction to convert the Canandaigua–Marion section of the trail into a road was completed in 1794.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The remainder of the trail north to Pultneyville was converted into a road approximately three years later.[[6]](#cite_note-marion-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/NY_21_original_northern_terminus.jpg/250px-NY_21_original_northern_terminus.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_21_original_northern_terminus.jpg)

The junction of Lake Road and Hamilton Street in [Pultneyville](/wiki/Pultneyville,_New_York "Pultneyville, New York"), which was NY 21's northern terminus from 1930 to 1980

The Canandaigua–Pultneyville highway served as a [post road](/wiki/Post_road "Post road") in the years that followed. At some point, the section of the highway between Palmyra and Marion, initially a [corduroy road](/wiki/Corduroy_road "Corduroy road"), was rebuilt as a [plank road](/wiki/Plank_road "Plank road") and tolled. The highway fell into disrepair over the years, leading travelers to conceive ways to avoid paying toll. The toll booths were eventually removed, and the road was later covered with dirt and converted into a [stage road](/wiki/Stage_road "Stage road").[[6]](#cite_note-marion-6) By the 1920s, the state of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") had assumed ownership of the Canandaigua–Pultneyville highway. In the mid-1920s, it became part of NY 72, a north–south highway extending from [NY 52](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_52_(1920s%E2%80%931930) "New York State Route 52 (1920s–1930)") in [Naples](/wiki/Naples_(village),_New_York "Naples (village), New York") to the Lake Ontario shoreline in Pultneyville by way of the western side of Canandaigua Lake.[[7]](#cite_note-1924nyt-7)[[8]](#cite_note-1926map-8)

### Establishment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_21&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Establishment")]

In the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"), NY 72 became part of NY 21, a new route extending from the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") at [Troupsburg](/wiki/Troupsburg,_New_York "Troupsburg, New York") to Pultneyville. It followed what is now [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") from the state line to [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York") and modern [County Route 121](/wiki/County_Route_121_(Steuben_County,_New_York) "County Route 121 (Steuben County, New York)") (CR 121), [NY 415](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_415 "New York State Route 415"), and [NY 371](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_371 "New York State Route 371") between Loon Lake and North Cohocton (via [Cohocton](/wiki/Cohocton_(village),_New_York "Cohocton (village), New York")). The remainder of NY 21 roughly followed its current alignment. At the same time, what is now NY 21 from [Andover](/wiki/Andover_(village),_New_York "Andover (village), New York") to [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York") was designated as the western half of [NY 17F](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17F "New York State Route 17F")[[2]](#cite_note-1930nyt-2) while the section between Loon Lake and modern NY 415 was assigned NY 371.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The remaining piece around [Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York") was part of [NY 2](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_2_(1927%E2%80%931938) "New York State Route 2 (1927–1938)") south of Wayland and part of the new [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39") from Wayland to Cohocton. NY 39 [overlapped](/wiki/Overlap_(road) "Overlap (road)") NY 21 from Cohocton to Naples, where it forked from NY 21 and continued east on modern [NY 245](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_245 "New York State Route 245").[[2]](#cite_note-1930nyt-2)

Several portions of current NY 21 south of Naples had carried designations prior to 1930. In 1924, the Andover–Hornell segment was designated as part of [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17"); at the same time, the short piece between modern NY 415 and the center of Wayland became part of [NY 4](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_4_(1924%E2%80%931927) "New York State Route 4 (1924–1927)"). By 1926, the portion connecting Wayland to Naples was included in the new NY 52.[[7]](#cite_note-1924nyt-7)[[8]](#cite_note-1926map-8) NY 4 was renumbered to NY 2 in 1927 to eliminate numerical duplication with the new [US 4](/wiki/U.S._Route_4_in_New_York "U.S. Route 4 in New York").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Realignments and truncations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_21&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Realignments and truncations")]

The alignments of NY 21 and NY 371 between Loon Lake and Cohocton were flipped c. 1937, routing NY 21 through the village of Wayland instead. The realignment extended NY 21's overlap with NY 39 west to Wayland and created an overlap with NY 2 south of the village.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) NY 2 was absorbed into an extended [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_New_York "U.S. Route 15 in New York") (now [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15")) by the following year.[[13]](#cite_note-1938gb-13) In the early 1940s, the NY 17F designation was eliminated and replaced with an extended [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") from Andover to Hornell.[[14]](#cite_note-1940map-14)[[15]](#cite_note-1942map-15) The alignments of NY 36 and NY 21 south of Hornell were flipped in the early 1950s, placing both routes on their current alignments south of the city.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Former_NY_21_in_Williamson.jpg/250px-Former_NY_21_in_Williamson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Former_NY_21_in_Williamson.jpg)

Lake Avenue (former NY 21) southbound at NY 21's current northern terminus in Williamson

On April 1, 1980, NY 21 was truncated to its present northern terminus at [NY 104](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_104 "New York State Route 104") in [Williamson](/wiki/Williamson,_New_York "Williamson, New York") as part of a highway maintenance swap between New York State and [Wayne County](/wiki/Wayne_County,_New_York "Wayne County, New York"). In the swap, ownership and maintenance of NY 21 north of Williamson was transferred from the state of New York to the county in exchange for maintenance of [NY 441](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_441 "New York State Route 441") between the [Monroe](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York")–Wayne county line and [NY 350](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_350 "New York State Route 350").[[18]](#cite_note-law-18) The former routing of NY 21 north to [Lake Road](/wiki/Lake_Road_(Western_New_York) "Lake Road (Western New York)") ([CR 101](/wiki/County_Route_101_(Wayne_County,_New_York) "County Route 101 (Wayne County, New York)")) in Pultneyville is now designated as CR 120.[[19]](#cite_note-pultneyville_quad-19)

Until the 1980s, NY 21 entered Canandaigua on Bristol Street and followed it to South Main Street (then-[US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5")), where it joined its current alignment. When the Western Bypass around the southwestern fringe of the city was constructed in the late 1970s and early 1980s, US 20 and NY 5 were realigned to follow the highway around the city. NY 21 was also realigned at this time to follow its modern routing on the bypass and South Main Street.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) The segment of Bristol Street vacated by NY 21 between the Western Bypass and the Canandaigua city line was maintained by the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") (NYSDOT) as unsigned NY 943A until September 1, 1996, when maintenance of the road was turned over to the town of [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(town),_New_York "Canandaigua (town), New York").[[18]](#cite_note-law-18)[[22]](#cite_note-22) However, the designation remained in NYSDOT documents until 2007.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-2007tdr-24)

## NY 21A

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_21&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: NY 21A")]

[NY 21A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21A "New York State Route 21A") was an alternate route of NY 21 along the eastern side of [Canandaigua Lake](/wiki/Canandaigua_Lake "Canandaigua Lake"). When it was assigned as part of the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"), it began at NY 21 north of the [village of Naples](/wiki/Naples_(village),_New_York "Naples (village), New York") and ran along the east shore of the lake to eastern [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(city),_New_York "Canandaigua (city), New York"), where it ended at [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") and [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5"). In between, NY 21A passed through the lakeside [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") of [Vine Valley](/wiki/Vine_Valley,_New_York "Vine Valley, New York").[[25]](#cite_note-1930map-25) The route was truncated c. 1939 to begin in Vine Valley, separating it from its parent route.[[13]](#cite_note-1938gb-13)[[26]](#cite_note-26) It was eliminated entirely in the early 1940s when most of NY 21A was absorbed by [NY 364](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_364 "New York State Route 364").[[14]](#cite_note-1940map-14)[[15]](#cite_note-1942map-15)
